name: Rolled Sea Chart
description: A weathered chart marked with coastlines, compass roses, and Atlantic routes, useful for navigators plotting passages between Europe, Africa, and the Americas.

name: Mariner's Pocket Compass
description: A brass pocket compass with a hinged lid, the kind a sailor, pilot, or explorer might carry to keep a steady bearing at sea.

name: Celestial Astrolabe
description: An ornate brass astrolabe used to measure the altitude of stars and the sun, helping mariners estimate latitude on long Atlantic voyages.

name: Sealed Captain's Letter
description: A folded parchment letter sealed in wax, carrying orders, reports, or private correspondence across ocean routes and colonial ports.

name: Captain's Logbook
description: A leather-bound maritime journal used to record winds, bearings, cargo, and daily events aboard an Atlantic vessel.

name: Brass Spyglass
description: A collapsible brass spyglass for scanning coastlines, distant sails, and harbor approaches during voyages of exploration and trade.

name: Cross-Staff
description: A wooden navigation instrument once used to measure the angle of celestial bodies, a practical tool for early modern seafarers.

name: Quill and Ink Set
description: A feather quill, parchment, and bottle of ink—essential for drafting journals, maps, ship logs, and colonial correspondence.

name: Navigation Dividers
description: Brass dividers used to measure distances on charts, favored by navigators and cartographers mapping the Atlantic world.

name: Merchant's Ledger
description: A worn leather ledger for recording debts, cargo, prices, and transactions in the bustling trade networks of the Atlantic economy.
